text: Hobbes’ model the mortal God fused into the immortal God, or as the title states, his Leviathan was a Commonwealth both ‘Civil and Ecclesiastical’. A theoretical point of a general relevance follows from this. European modernity as it developed from the seventeenth century is normally understood as a separation of secular from sacred. In most prevailing theories, European modernity is characterized by secularization of the diverse sub-fields of human thought and action. This separation of sacred and secular can then be identified with the rise of the secular polity, as it grew out of the absolute state. On the one hand, there is an obvious element of truth in these accounts. Scientific rationalization, abstraction and homogenization of time and space and new techniques of measuring and calculating were indeed developments followed by political rationalization and bureaucratization, epitomized in the centralist, absolutist state. Yet the common view of political modernity as a process of differentiation of politics from religion must be modified, for what happens is also quite the opposite. The historical novelty of Hobbes’ construct is rather that state and Church come to stand in such a close relationship that any contradiction or tension between temporal and spiritual becomes meaningless (Voegelin 2000: 53). The political model of Hobbes – that was to become the model of the modern state, sealing itself off against other modern states – did not separate out divinity. It took away its autonomy by standing so close to it that it could draw directly from its primary truth. Hobbes’ model was peculiar, not for clearing the ground by separating sacred from secular, but for bringing them so close that the tension ceased to be meaningful on the level of thought and social organization. If this is a process of ‘secularization’, it is so only insofar as it manages to hide its own origins. 16 The Illusion of ‘Absolutism’? We have argued so far that Descartes and Hobbes both created a distinct and novel type of ‘absolutism’ in the form of a strongly demarcated boundary around 16 Or, as Pizzorno (1987) argues, if absolute politics were indeed based on the historical dissociation of the spiritual and the temporal realms, this process should not be confounded with what is generally seen as a process of secularization of politics. Liminality and the Modern 132 either self or state, with strong claims to self-foundation and with parallel types of symbolism. At this point, the objection to the above account of statehood and personhood might be that the idea of absolutism was a myth from the beginning: it never ‘happened’, either within thought, or within political rule. Indeed, such a criticism has been part and parcel of modern philosophy since Descartes. The possible objection is therefore that the analysis suggested neglects the fierce critiques of Cartesian rationalism. Did not Pascal blame Descartes for ‘wanting to do without God’, and was not this reaction constitutive of modernity? This is not the question but the answer. Within the interpretative space of modernity (Wagner 2008), critiques could be raised only insofar as they set themselves against the project of rationality. Similar objections have been launched against Hobbes’ model of human nature. Montesquieu and Rousseau had an easy time showing that Hobbes’ man was indeed nothing but a peculiar history- specific image transferred to the level of humanity. ‘Nature’ or ‘culture’ in some form or the other were then launched against Hobbesian or Cartesian rationality. Rationality became something to hate or love, attack or defend. Rousseau aimed to show that nature could not be kept out of self and society, or that it ought not be kept out. Freud argued that Culture without libido equalled repression of the Id (his version of human nature) and led to an unbalanced Ego. Such attempts to counter ‘too much rationality’, however, share the predictable outcome that they reinforce the divide they set out to overcome. This is especially so as the natural or cultural forces they set loose against the forces of reason are in themselves quite ‘un-ordered’, with no real alternative to the excess of order. What unites Pascal, Rousseau, Nietzsche, Freud and most other sceptics of modernity is that they proposed a new kind of model in which ‘passions’, ‘sentiments’, ‘will- power’, or ‘drives’, would once again enter the world. However, these celebrations of rule-transcending self-assertion or boundless self-expression would never succeed in creating any real connection to reflexive self-formation through self- restraint upon which society could be positively re-imagined. The reduced and impoverished self left behind by Descartes and Hobbes would remain as a residual category, re-injected with passions of whatever kind. However effective these critiques were, and however much they jointly came to model modernity and the modern personality, the kind of self they set out to defend could not possibly serve as a new source of a positive order – as the rationalists would easily respond. And so the discussion reached a stalemate. The mainstream debates that characterized the 1980s and 1990s between postmodernists and modernists only served to underline that fact. In similar ways, the absolute model of the state has undergone criticism since its inception, and not only from a religious perspective. It has today become increasingly frequent to question the sovereignty of the European state as it came into being from the Peace of Westphalia in 1648. Political theorists have shown how in practice the autonomy of the state was never absolute (see for example Krasner 1995). In the present period where the very concept of state sovereignty Liminality in the Transition to Modernity 133 is seriously brought into question, this tendency to de-absolutize the state back into history is understandably widespread. State power remained inwardly and outwardly contested from 1648, relying on international power configurations, then as now. The argument can be pressed by showing that with the very rise of absolutism the state was in fact becoming increasingly ruled by the logics of a globalizing economy. From this perspective, absolutism was the (false) ideology of state power that
